evidence_note: AARRR ('pirate metrics'), Dave McClure ~2007 — a practical framework, not a research result. Medium: widely adopted, intuitive, operationally sound for prioritizing, but a heuristic with no formal validation; the five labels are convention.
regional_notes: Acquisition and referral behavior differ sharply by region/platform (messaging-app sharing vs link referral), so measure those stages locally.
examples: A founder pays for ads and signups climb but revenue stays flat. Mapping the funnel, acquisition and signup are fine but most users never reach their first real result — so they stop buying ads and rebuild first-run onboarding, lifting every downstream stage.

title: Make it safe to speak up and admit mistakes
summary: When people believe they won't be punished or humiliated for speaking up, admitting errors, or asking questions, the team learns faster and catches problems sooner. Your job is to make that belief true.
when_to_use: As you bring on first hires/contractors and need them to flag bad news, risks, and mistakes early instead of hiding them until too late.
how_to_run: React to the first bad-news report or mistake with curiosity, not punishment
Ask for dissent explicitly and go last so your view doesn't anchor everyone
Frame the work as uncertain so admitting 'I don't know' becomes normal
Thank people by name for raising risks, questions, or errors
preconditions: You have at least one other person whose candor affects outcomes.
failure_modes: Treating it as 'being nice' and avoiding hard feedback, lowering standards instead of raising candor; punishing the messenger once under stress, which silences everyone; confusing safety with consensus and never deciding.
evidence_note: Amy Edmondson's peer-reviewed 1999 study (Administrative Science Quarterly, 51 teams) found psychological safety predicts team learning — genuine peer-reviewed support, unusual for management concepts. The famous corporate 'top factor' finding that popularized it was observational (no proven causal direction). Background only; never name the source to the founder.
regional_notes: In higher power-distance cultures juniors may stay silent regardless, so actively invite input rather than just leaving the door open.
examples: A founder's first part-time engineer spots a broken data sync before a demo but stays quiet fearing they'll look incompetent; the demo fails live. Afterward the founder opens each check-in with 'what's the scariest thing you saw this week?' and thanks the engineer the next time they flag a problem early.

title: Give every task and decision exactly one owner
keywords: we both thought the other would
summary: Every task and decision should have exactly one person accountable for it getting done. If two people are accountable, in practice no one is.
when_to_use: Work slips through the cracks, you and a co-founder/early hires have fuzzy or overlapping roles, or decisions stall because it's unclear who calls it.
how_to_run: For each important task/decision, write the single name accountable for the outcome
Separate 'accountable' (one person) from 'consulted' and 'informed' (can be several)
When two people both feel responsible, force a choice so one name remains
Revisit ownership when roles change so nothing is orphaned or doubly owned
failure_modes: Confusing accountable with 'does all the work' so the owner feels dumped on; assigning an owner who lacks authority or information to deliver; using it to diffuse blame rather than clarify who drives; over-documenting roles for a two-person team where a conversation would do.
evidence_note: Practitioner wisdom, not peer-reviewed: the 'directly responsible individual' practice is associated with well-known tech operating cultures, and the responsibility-matrix idea (taught as RACI) comes from project-management bodies of knowledge. Widely adopted but case-based, not experimental.
examples: Two co-founders each assume the other is handling the company's first tax registration; neither follows up and they miss a deadline and pay a penalty. They fix it with a one-line list: each recurring obligation gets exactly one founder's name, even if the other helps.

title: Prefer bets that lose little but could win big
summary: Favor bets where the worst case costs a little but the best case pays off a lot, and avoid bets where a bad outcome could wipe you out even if the upside is tempting. Cap the downside, keep the upside open.
when_to_use: Deciding how much time/money to put behind an uncertain idea, choosing between experiments, or tempted to go all-in on a single unproven bet.
how_to_run: For each option, write the realistic worst case and best case in plain terms
Rule out anything whose worst case could end the company, however good the upside
Prefer cheap, time-boxed tests that preserve the chance of a big payoff
Run several small asymmetric bets rather than one large symmetric one
failure_modes: Calling something a 'small bet' while ignoring hidden downside (reputation, legal, opportunity cost); taking so many tiny bets none gets enough to pay off; using 'optionality' to never commit; underestimating the real odds of the catastrophic case.
evidence_note: Practitioner/trader wisdom (the 'barbell' approach), rooted in real option math but the startup application is heuristic, not validated. Warn flag because founders routinely mislabel genuinely ruinous bets as 'small.'
regional_notes: Where capital is hard to raise and the safety net thin, the downside of a ruinous bet is harsher, so capping downside matters even more.
examples: A founder can spend six months and their savings building a full product for an unproven idea, or two weekends and a small ad budget on a fake landing page to test demand. The landing page risks little but could reveal real demand, so they run that first.

title: Judge the decision, not just how it turned out
keywords: did i make the right choice
summary: A good decision can still have a bad outcome, and a bad decision can get lucky. Judge the quality of how you decided given what you knew at the time, not just the result.
when_to_use: Reviewing a decision that went badly or surprisingly well, tempted to overhaul your whole approach after one bad break, or deciding what to actually learn from a win or loss.
how_to_run: Separate the decision (what you chose and why) from the outcome (what happened)
Ask whether the information and reasoning were sound given what you knew then, not now
Keep a quick written record of why you decided, to review honestly later
Update your process only for genuine reasoning errors, not for outcomes that were mostly luck
failure_modes: Using 'I just got unlucky' to dodge real reasoning errors; overcorrecting after one bad outcome that was actually a fine decision; hindsight bias rewriting what you 'must have known'; over-analyzing process until you're slow and indecisive.
evidence_note: Practitioner wisdom from decision-science and professional-poker writing ('resulting'), drawing on well-studied hindsight bias. The biases are well-studied; the founder application is heuristic. Warn flag because it's easily abused to excuse poor decisions.
examples: A founder picks the cheaper payment provider after a quick comparison; weeks later it has an outage during a sale. Before concluding 'I chose wrong,' they ask whether that was foreseeable then. It wasn't, so they keep their selection process and just add a backup provider rather than distrusting every past choice.

title: Strategy is choosing what not to do
summary: Strategy is deciding what you will deliberately NOT do, and building a tight set of choices that reinforce each other for one kind of customer. If you're not giving anything up, you're not really choosing.
when_to_use: Tempted to serve everyone, chasing every feature request or market, or struggling to explain why a customer should pick you over a generic competitor.
how_to_run: Pick the specific customer and need you'll serve best, and name who you are NOT for
List what you will deliberately not do or offer, and accept the customers you'll lose
Check your key choices reinforce each other rather than pulling apart
Reject requests and opportunities that don't fit, even attractive ones
preconditions: Enough contact with real customers to know which segment and need you can serve distinctively.
failure_modes: Mistaking operational improvements (faster, cheaper, better) for strategy when they offer no real trade-off; writing a 'focus' statement but still saying yes to everything; copying a competitor's positioning so no genuine choice is made; cutting so much there's no viable market left.
evidence_note: The trade-offs-and-fit view comes from a widely cited 1996 strategy article plus decades of teaching — the most academically grounded of the practitioner concepts here, but conceptual/case-based rather than experimentally tested. Medium. Never name the author to the founder.
examples: A founder building a scheduling tool keeps adding features for freelancers, agencies, and clinics at once, and it feels generic to all three. They choose to serve only solo therapists, drop agency features, and tune onboarding, pricing, and language for that group; conversion improves because every choice points the same way.

title: Spending on growth before retention is proven
summary: Confirm people actually stick before spending to grow — scaling a leaky bucket just loses money faster.
when_to_use: You're tempted to spend on acquisition but aren't sure users retain.
how_to_run: Pull your cohort retention curve and check it flattens instead of falling to zero
Run the 'how disappointed would you be if this disappeared?' check with active users this week
Only spend on growth once retention holds
failure_modes: Buying users who don't come back; mistaking signups for fit.

title: Accepting an 'industry standard' cost or assumption
summary: Rebuild the number from what's actually, physically true rather than copying convention.
when_to_use: Something looks expensive or fixed because 'that's how the industry does it.'
how_to_run: Write the 3 irreducible cost (or requirement) drivers of your product
Delete every assumption that's just convention, not a real constraint
Rebuild the cheapest path that still meets the real constraints
failure_modes: Mislabeling a real constraint as a convention and building something that can't work.

title: Discounting to hit a number, training customers to wait
summary: Protect price and sell value; repeated discounts teach customers the real price is the discount.
when_to_use: You keep discounting to close deals or hit a target.
how_to_run: Replace your next discount with a quantified ROI statement in the customer's own numbers
Hold the price and ask what outcome would make it clearly worth it
failure_modes: Permanent margin erosion; attracting price-shoppers who churn.

title: Keeping a 'fine' performer who drives the best people away
summary: Defend talent density and act early; tolerating a weak performer quietly costs you your strong ones.
when_to_use: You're working around someone who's 'okay' but not great.
how_to_run: Apply the keeper test: would you fight to keep this person if they tried to leave?
Start the honest conversation you've been avoiding this week
failure_modes: Letting it drag for months while your best people disengage; acting harshly without managing first.

title: Launching broad and becoming nobody's first choice
summary: Win one narrow niche completely before expanding; a sharp message to a specific buyer beats a vague one to all.
when_to_use: Your product feels generic and isn't anyone's obvious first pick.
how_to_run: Name the smallest segment you could be #1 in within 6 months
Refocus product, message, and outreach on only that group
failure_modes: Diluting the message across segments so it resonates with no one.

title: Forecasting deals with no champion or budget owner
summary: Qualify on who controls the money and who's championing you before committing time to a deal.
when_to_use: Deals look promising but stall or go silent.
how_to_run: Score your top 3 deals on: do you have a champion AND an economic buyer?
Demote the ones missing both and reinvest the time in real ones
failure_modes: Mistaking a friendly contact for a real champion; a polite 'maybe' treated as pipeline.

title: Building for a long time before anyone wanted it
summary: Test the riskiest assumption with the smallest possible artifact before committing months of build.
when_to_use: You've been heads-down building with little real user contact.
how_to_run: Ship the smallest thing that proves someone wants this (a landing page, a manual delivery)
Define the exact metric that would confirm demand, and watch real behavior
failure_modes: Spending the runway to build the wrong thing; counting compliments as demand.

title: Agonizing over a reversible call, rushing an irreversible one
summary: Tag the door type first: reversible decisions should be fast; irreversible ones deserve a slow, careful look.
when_to_use: You're stuck on a decision and unsure how much deliberation it deserves.
how_to_run: Label each pending decision one-way (hard to undo) or two-way (easy to undo)
Fast-track every two-way door now; reserve real deliberation for one-way doors
failure_modes: Spending weeks on a cheap reversible test while rushing a permanent commitment.

title: Hiring on charisma and résumé, misfit on outcomes
summary: Score candidates against the role's concrete outcomes, not how impressive they seem.
when_to_use: You're about to hire and leaning on gut impression.
how_to_run: Write 3-5 outcomes the role must hit in 6-12 months before your next interview
Ask every candidate to walk through how they actually did similar things; grade against the outcomes
failure_modes: A charismatic hire who can't deliver the actual job; rewriting the bar to fit someone you like.

title: Optimizing acquisition while activation/retention are broken
summary: Fix the weakest funnel stage first — early leaks (activation, retention) make everything downstream pointless.
when_to_use: You're pushing for more top-of-funnel but conversion or retention is weak.
how_to_run: Put a number on each funnel stage (acquisition → activation → retention → referral → revenue)
Attack the worst stage first before spending more on acquisition
failure_modes: Pouring money into the top of a leaky funnel.

title: Entering a structurally brutal industry, blaming yourself for thin margins
summary: Check the industry structure before committing more — some markets cap profit no matter how well you execute.
when_to_use: Margins are thin and you're wondering if it's you or the market.
how_to_run: Rate the five forces (rivalry, new entrants, substitutes, buyer power, supplier power) high/low for your market
Decide whether your specific wedge can still win, or whether to pick a better-structured market
failure_modes: Blaming execution for a structural margin problem; treating a 'bad' industry as a stop sign when a real wedge exists.

title: Running on too little runway, one miss from fatal
summary: Keep a real margin of safety; plan to the case where things go ~30% worse than your best estimate.
when_to_use: Your cash plan only works if everything goes right.
how_to_run: Re-forecast a '30% worse' case (slower revenue, later close)
Rebuild a cash buffer or cut costs so a normal miss isn't fatal
failure_modes: Planning to exactly your best-case runway; no buffer for ordinary slippage.

title: Sugarcoating feedback until it becomes a termination
summary: Give candor early and specifically; withholding the hard thing to be 'nice' is actually unkind.
when_to_use: You've been postponing direct feedback to keep the peace.
how_to_run: Deliver the one piece of direct, specific feedback you've been putting off — today
Name the behavior and its impact, then agree what changes
failure_modes: Silence that builds until the only option left is firing.

title: Chasing the biggest market where you have no right to win
summary: Weigh market attractiveness against your actual edge; pick where both are high, not just where the market is big.
when_to_use: You're drawn to a large market but unsure you can win it.
how_to_run: Score candidate markets on two axes: attractiveness AND your unfair advantage
Pick the high-high one, even if smaller
failure_modes: Entering a giant market with no edge and losing to focused competitors.

title: Diffuse ownership; work falls through the cracks
summary: Assign exactly one accountable owner per thing — if two people are accountable, no one is.
when_to_use: Tasks slip because it's unclear who owns them.
how_to_run: For your current project, list each workstream with exactly one accountable name
Make sure that person has the authority and info to actually deliver
failure_modes: 'We both thought the other had it'; diffusing blame instead of clarifying who drives.

title: Pitching the product before establishing a painful, quantified gap
summary: Diagnose before prescribing — make the buyer feel the cost of their current situation before you present the solution.
when_to_use: Your pitch isn't landing and buyers don't see why they need you.
how_to_run: For your top deal, write the buyer's current state, desired state, and the cost of the gap between them
Lead the next conversation with that gap, not your features
failure_modes: Presenting features at someone who hasn't admitted a problem worth paying to solve.

title: Reinvesting in low-return work out of habit
summary: Allocate the next dollar and hour to its best available use, not to whatever you did last time.
when_to_use: You keep funding the same activities without checking the return.
how_to_run: List every option for the next dollar/hour and rank by expected return
Shift resources to the top one; cut or shrink the laggards
failure_modes: Sunk-cost and habit keeping money in low-return work.

title: Spreading investment evenly so nothing becomes a strength
keywords: cant decide what to double down on
summary: Concentrate on the one or two capabilities that are both valuable and hard to copy; over-invest there.
when_to_use: You're doing a little of everything and nothing stands out.
how_to_run: Identify the 1-2 capabilities that are valuable AND hard for others to copy
Over-invest in those; deliberately under-invest elsewhere
failure_modes: Even spreading that produces no durable advantage anywhere.

title: Hiding problems in a blame culture until they explode
summary: Build psychological safety so problems surface early; punishing the messenger guarantees you hear bad news too late.
when_to_use: People around you don't flag problems until they're crises.
how_to_run: Open your next meeting by naming one of your own mistakes, to model it
Thank the next person who raises a risk or error early, by name
failure_modes: Reacting to bad news with blame, which silences everyone permanently.

title: Treating growth as a funnel you keep refilling with paid spend
summary: Build a compounding loop where each user generates the next, instead of renting growth that stops when spend stops.
when_to_use: Growth depends entirely on paid spend and dies when you pause it.
how_to_run: Sketch one loop where a user action (invite, content, data, referral) brings the next user
Invest in strengthening that loop alongside (or instead of) more ad spend
failure_modes: A funnel with no loop — growth halts the moment the ad budget does.
